author: @poseidon201
text: Great video, I didn't know about the fine filler either, I always end up with little recesses, especially on bigger holes, which you can still see when top coated. Great tip, cheers.

author: @stevetobias4890
text:
Great video, often when you get a really nice piece of reclaimed timber painting is usually a non-preferred option. Varnish is better when the wood is that good. For this make your own filler with a mix of fine sanding dust and wood glue, when you have dark areas caused by woodgrain you can use sanding dust from another wood type if you take your time the finish will be unnoticeable. 
Sometimes you can even use a commercial wood filler for stock wood types. When painting you are spot on the money Luke, fine dents can sometimes be soaked out by wetting the wood to allow the wood grain to swell.

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Thank you for the tip mate! I like the idea of homemade filler from wood shavings and glue, I will definitely try this. Cheers mate! :)

author: @uncledodge9396
text: You do this the same way as I do, however with some larger holes I tend to drill them out to either 6mm or 10mm and then glue in a wooden dowel, then obviously sand back, Keep the videos coming fella, you're doing well.

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: That is a great idea! I might have to use that. Cheers mate! :)

author: @jamescunliffe9872
text: If I was attaching it to something I'd just flip it over!;)

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Hahahah fair point! It depends on what I'm doing really. I do like to make sure everything looks good before i build something lol. Cheers mate! :)

author: @mikebond6328
text: Thanks for that. I didn’t know about the fine filler. Now I know why I always have a little dimple when I use wood filler.

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: It happened to me every time lol. Now i know haha. Cheers mate! :)

author: @WayneCarolan
text: Great video and content Luke, you’re doing so well on front of the camera. I’d say the editing took some time?

author: @RocketSteve
text: Thanks, you just reminded me I need to take some wood filler to mums to finish building work. Cheers!

author: @alaingrignon
text: Great short vid on this subject. I recently made a video myself on how I used this method to smooth out particle board into a nice smooth surface! I would only caution that, though the poly fill stuff works wonders on filling holes into a smooth surface, if you're intention for filling the whole is to redrill new holes into the wood or screwing new screws into it, I would suggest using epoxy mixed with sawdust collected from sanding the same piece of wood you intend to fill the holes with, this will best approximate the same wood color in your epoxy fill.

author: @awuk3468
text: Good video, if you have larger holes you can always glue other bits of wood inside the hole before filling, I can tell your confidence level is increasing the more videos you do,  keep it up and you'll be a natural in no time!

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Great suggestion, that would defiantly make it easier to fill.  Thanks for the tip mate! :)

author: @GREATLORDPOOH
text: After the first fill whip with a damp cloth instead of sanding
